project.name: zalo-sales-crm
project.description: ZaloCRM v3.3 — centralized web platform to manage multiple personal Zalo accounts: real-time chat (Socket.io), media via MinIO/S3/R2, Facebook lead ingestion, automation/bot framework, lead scoring, RBAC, and PWA. Analyzed on the cayenne worktree (main + further dev).

nodes.name: use-org-timezone.ts
nodes.summary: Vue composable and standalone helpers for formatting and computing dates in the organization's configured timezone, using fixed-offset parsing rather than the Intl API.
nodes.languageNotes: Implements timezone shifting via UTC millisecond offsets parsed from a '+HH:MM' string, then reads UTC getters on the shifted Date to avoid Intl.DateTimeFormat overhead.

layers.description: Pinia stores, Vue composables, axios API clients, router, constants, plugins, and global styles driving client-side state and backend communication.

layers.description: Fastify route handlers, serializers, and auth/RBAC/zalo-access middleware exposing HTTP and socket endpoints across all feature modules.

layers.description: Core business logic: automation engine, lead scoring, analytics, AI prompt services, friend-sync, scheduled cron/worker jobs, and the app bootstrap.

layers.name: Backend Integrations
layers.description: External connectivity: the multi-account Zalo pool/SDK/socket/listener stack, Facebook/Telegram/Zapier/Google-Sheets connectors, and the sync engine.

layers.description: The Prisma schema, SQL migration files, and database table/schema nodes defining ZaloCRM's relational model.

layers.name: Shared Utilities
layers.description: Cross-cutting backend helpers: crypto/AES-GCM, Prisma/Redis/MinIO clients, SSRF guard, video processor, phone normalization, serializers, and logging.

layers.name: Infrastructure & Config
layers.description: Dockerfiles, compose stacks and service definitions, dev/deploy/migration scripts, PWA assets, and build configs (tsconfig, vite, vitest, package.json).

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: Start with the README to understand what ZaloCRM is: a multi-account Zalo CRM combining real-time chat, MinIO/S3/R2 media storage, Facebook lead ingestion, an automation/bot framework, lead scoring, RBAC, and PWA delivery. This document frames the major subsystems and how the frontend and backend halves fit together, giving you the map for the rest of the tour.

tour.title: Frontend Boot & Routing
tour.description: The Vue client boots from main.ts, which mounts the root App.vue shell, registers the Vuetify UI plugin, and wires the router. The router defines the authenticated page tree (chat workspace, CRM dashboards, automation builder, RBAC/admin), so reading these together shows how a request to any URL resolves into a rendered view.
tour.languageLesson: A Vue 3 app is created with createApp(App) and extended via .use(plugin) for the router and Vuetify before .mount(). Each plugin installs globally available functionality (routing, components, theming) into the single app instance.

tour.title: Backend Server Bootstrap
tour.description: On the server side, app.ts assembles the Express/Fastify application: it loads configuration, registers middleware, and mounts the feature route modules (chat, zalo, automation, auth) you will explore later. This is the backend's table of contents — it shows every subsystem's HTTP surface in one place and how cross-cutting concerns like auth are applied.

tour.title: Data Model
tour.description: The Prisma schema is the single most depended-upon node in the graph (highest fan-in) — nearly every backend module reads or writes through it. It defines the core entities: organizations, users, Zalo accounts, contacts, conversations, messages, automation campaigns, and scoring tables. Understanding these relationships now makes every later module easier to follow, since the code speaks in terms of these models.
tour.languageLesson: Prisma models declare fields with scalar and relation types; @relation links foreign keys, and the generated client gives type-safe queries. The schema is the source of truth — migrations under prisma/migrations evolve the database to match it incrementally.

tour.title: Real-Time Chat Backend
tour.description: Building on the message and conversation models from Step 4, the chat backend exposes REST endpoints in chat-routes and processes incoming/outgoing messages through message-handler. The handler normalizes message payloads, persists them, and pushes them onward — connecting the HTTP layer to the live Zalo socket you will see next.

tour.title: Real-Time Chat Frontend
tour.description: The client mirror of Step 5: the use-chat composable holds conversation and message state and subscribes to socket events, while MessageThread.vue renders the scrolling thread, reactions, and reply UI. Together they turn the backend's persisted-and-broadcast messages into a live, reactive chat experience.
tour.languageLesson: Vue composables (functions named use-*) encapsulate reactive state and side effects using ref/reactive and lifecycle hooks, then return that state to any component — the idiomatic way to share logic without mixins in the Composition API.

tour.title: Zalo Integration Layer
tour.description: The heart of the product: zalo-pool manages a pool of authenticated Zalo accounts (via zca-js sessions), zalo-routes exposes account and messaging operations over HTTP, and zalo-socket streams live events back into the chat pipeline from Step 5. This layer abstracts the unofficial Zalo client so the rest of the app can treat many accounts as one uniform interface.

tour.title: Automation & Bot Framework
tour.description: automation-service orchestrates campaigns and sequences, while action-dispatcher routes each automation step to a concrete action handler (send-message, request-friend, update-status) that ultimately calls the Zalo layer from Step 7. This is how scheduled broadcasts and rule-driven bot replies are executed without manual operator action.

tour.title: Facebook Lead Ingestion & Lead Scoring
tour.description: Leads enter the CRM from outside: facebook-lead-worker pulls Lead Ads form submissions and lead-scoring/score-engine assign each contact a quality score from behavioral signals. New contacts created here flow into the same contact and conversation models from Step 4, feeding the chat and automation pipelines.

tour.title: Auth, RBAC & Privacy
tour.description: Every route mounted in Step 3 is gated by this layer: auth-service issues sessions, auth-middleware verifies them, and role-middleware plus rbac-middleware enforce department- and permission-group-based access. On the client, the auth Pinia store holds the session and drives the router guards from Step 2, so the UI only shows what a user is permitted to see.

tour.title: Containerization & Deployment
tour.description: The capstone: the multi-stage Dockerfile builds the frontend and backend separately then assembles a lean runtime image, while docker-compose wires the app together with its Postgres database, Redis, and MinIO object store — the services that back the data model (Step 4) and media storage. This shows how every subsystem in the tour runs together in production.
tour.languageLesson: Multi-stage Docker builds use separate FROM stages (frontend-builder, backend-builder, then a slim runtime node stage) so compilers and dev dependencies stay out of the final image. docker-compose ties services together with depends_on ordering, named volumes for persistent Postgres/MinIO data, and an internal network for service-to-service DNS.
